title: Research Projects
text:
Master’s Thesis — Construct Validity of Socio-Emotional Skills (Ongoing)

Working Title: Old Wine in New Bottles? Construct Validation of Socio-Emotional Skills.
Topic: Current educational research increasingly conceptualizes socio-emotional and behavioral skills (SEB skills) as malleable, functional capacities — as something individuals can do. In contrast, personality traits describe relatively stable behavioral tendencies — what individuals are like. My thesis examines whether this distinction is empirically tenable or whether the skill concept partly reflects a linguistic re-labeling of stable traits, commonly discussed as jangle fallacy or trait re-labeling.
Theoretical Framework: The project is grounded in a construct-validation perspective and uses Kane’s Interpretation-Use Argument to systematically evaluate different lines of validity evidence. The central aim is to clarify whether SEB skills provide added conceptual and empirical value beyond established personality constructs.
Research Focus: Beyond examining rank-order stability in general, the thesis investigates whether SEB skills show differential stability under the influence of an exogenous contextual change. Specifically, school-track transitions, such as moving from lower or comprehensive secondary schooling into the academic track, are treated as naturally occurring contextual perturbations that may affect the development and relative ordering of socio-emotional skills.
Research Questions:

How stable is the factorial structure of SEB skills across everyday school contexts?
Are SEB skills more susceptible than personality traits to changes in rank-order stability following an exogenous contextual transition, such as entering the academic school track?
Are high correlations between skills and traits a theoretical feature, reflecting reciprocal development, or a methodological bug, reflecting semantic redundancy and self-report artifacts?

Methodological Approach:

Validation Framework: Application of Kane’s Interpretation-Use Argument to structure the validity evidence.
Modeling: Use of Exploratory Structural Equation Modeling (ESEM) to represent the fuzzy structure of the BESSI-G framework.
Structural Analyses: Examination of empirical distinctness through latent autoregressive models of rank-order stability and bifactor models to separate general factors from methodological artifacts.
Reproducibility: Implementation of the analyses in a fully reproducible workflow using R and Quarto.

Status: Ongoing master’s thesis project.



Research Project — Human–LLM Coding of Metacognitive Support in Mathematics Classrooms

Topic: Evaluating the accuracy of LLM-based coding of metacognitive support in mathematics classroom transcripts.
Content: In this project, we code mathematics classroom transcripts using the Metacognitive Support Framework by Zepeda and colleagues. The framework is used to identify and categorize instances of metacognitive support in teacher-student interaction across theoretically defined dimensions.
Research Focus: We compare human codings with codings generated by LLaMA, a large language model, using structured reasoning prompts. The project examines the extent to which the model can identify relevant transcript passages and assign them to the same metacognitive support dimensions as human coders.
Key Contribution: The project contributes to the methodological discussion on whether large language models can support theory-driven qualitative coding in educational research. Rather than treating automated coding as a black box, we evaluate its agreement and accuracy against human coding decisions.
Methods:

Theory-driven coding of classroom transcripts based on the Metacognitive Support Framework by Zepeda and colleagues.
LLM-based identification and classification of relevant transcript passages using LLaMA and structured reasoning prompts.
Comparison of human and model-based codings across framework dimensions.
Evaluation of agreement and accuracy between human coders and the LLM output.

title: Projects
text:
Master’s Thesis (ongoing) — Conceptual & Psychometric Foundations of Socio-Emotional Skills (BESSI)


Topic: Psychometric validation of a short-form instrument to assess socio-emotional skills in adolescents.


Content: In my master’s thesis, I investigate the conceptual and psychometric foundations of socio-emotional skills measurement, focusing on the distinction between skills (“I can”) and personality traits (“I am”). The project examines whether high correlations reflect substantive overlap or near-synonymous item wording.


Methods:



Large longitudinal sample of upper secondary students


Exploratory Structural Equation Modeling (ESEM)


Longitudinal measurement invariance testing


Latent stability models


R (lavaan), FIML for missing data





Secondary Analysis — ITT & CACE effects in a digital socio-emotional intervention


Topic: Compliance-sensitive causal effects in a digital socio-emotional intervention for preschool children.


Content: Secondary analysis of a randomized controlled trial. Beyond intention-to-treat (ITT), I applied Complier Average Causal Effect (CACE) models to examine how intervention effects depend on actual usage (compliance).


Methods:



Secondary analysis of an RCT


ITT vs. CACE comparison


FIML for missing data


Sensitivity and tipping-point analyses



Key contribution: ITT effects were small/absent, while CACE estimates suggested stronger effects among high-compliance participants and highlighted threats to causal interpretation when design assumptions are weak.




Experimental Study — Epistemic emotions and information-seeking


Topic: Epistemic emotions, misinformation, and exploratory information-seeking.


Content: In an online experiment, we tested whether stylistic framings of refutation texts induce epistemic emotions (confusion vs. situational interest) and influence exploratory information-seeking behaviour and comprehension.


Methods:



Online experiment (between-subjects)


Manipulation via text design


Behavioural measure (click behaviour)


Regression models, ANOVA, robustness checks



Key contribution: No clear condition differences, but the study provides a methodological analysis of limits in emotion induction and click-based proxies.




Bachelor’s Thesis — Mediation model of mindfulness, decentering, and emotional competence


Topic: Do decentering and emotional competence mediate the relationship between mindfulness and well-being?


Content: In my bachelor’s thesis, I examined whether the relationship between mindfulness and well-being can be explained by underlying mechanisms, specifically decentering and emotional competence. Using cross-sectional survey data, I tested a mediation model to explore potential indirect pathways.


Methods:



Online survey study (N = 190)


Mediation analysis using PROCESS (Hayes)


Bootstrapping (5,000 iterations)


Path analysis (SPSS AMOS)


A priori power analysis



Key contribution: While causal interpretation was limited due to the correlational design, the project introduced a mechanistic perspective on socio-emotional competencies and highlighted the need for experimental and compliance-sensitive approaches in subsequent work.

title: Likertitems als metrisch oder ordinalskaliert betrachten?
text:

Schätzung mit Weighted Least Squares Mean and Variance adjustedund in Sewell et al. (2024) |
Vergleich mit Maximum Likelihood (ML) Schätzung, wenn die Faktorladungen relativ gleichbleiben/ Standardfehler nicht zu sehr voneinander abweichen –> weiterrechnen mit ML für weitere Analysen.

title: About
text:
Master’s Student | Empirical Educational Research & Educational Psychology
University of Tübingen (Expected graduation: October 2026)
My research interests lie at the intersection of educational psychology, psychometrics, and intervention research. I am particularly interested in socio-emotional competencies and self-regulation: how these constructs can be conceptualized, measured validly, and translated into targeted educational interventions.
A central question in my work is how we can move beyond purely self-report-based assessments toward more objective and context-sensitive methods of measuring socio-emotional and self-regulatory skills. I am interested in how they develop in educational contexts, and how interventions can foster them in meaningful and causally interpretable ways.
Methodologically, I work with quantitative and qualitative approaches, including longitudinal measurement models, psychometric validation, secondary analyses of randomized interventions (CACE), and theory-driven coding of classroom interactions.






Socio-emotional competencies and self-regulation are central to educational success, well-being, and adaptive development. However, their measurement often faces a validity problem: many instruments rely heavily on self-report and may blur the distinction between skills, traits, and context-dependent behavior.
I am interested in closing this gap by developing and evaluating more valid, context-sensitive, and objective approaches to measurement. This includes investigating whether we are measuring what learners can do, what they are like, or how they respond to specific educational environments.
At the same time, I am interested in how socio-emotional and self-regulatory skills can be deliberately supported through educational interventions. My long-term interest is to connect valid measurement with intervention design: understanding not only whether an intervention works, but also what exactly changes, for whom, and under which conditions.



Across my projects, I aim to align substantive research questions with appropriate measurement strategies and analytic approaches. My methodological interests include psychometric validation, longitudinal modeling, causal inference in applied intervention research, and the use of qualitative and automated coding approaches to capture complex educational processes.
I am especially interested in measurement approaches that go beyond retrospective self-report, such as behavioral indicators, contextualized assessments, classroom discourse analysis, and digital or simulation-based assessment formats.



I aim to pursue a PhD in which I can contribute to making socio-emotional and self-regulatory development more validly measurable and more effectively actionable in educational contexts.
My goal is to move beyond broad claims about “non-cognitive skills” toward a more precise understanding of how these competencies can be assessed, fostered, and evaluated: what works, for whom, why, and under which contextual conditions.

title: About
text: While socio-emotional and self-regulatory skills are key to educational success, their measurement often faces a ‘validity gap.’ I am interested in closing this gap by investigating whether we are measuring stable traits or malleable skills—and how we can design interventions that lead to robust, causally interpretable improvements.

I am especially interested in measurement approaches that go beyond retrospective self-report, such as behavioral indicators, contextualized assessments, classroom discourse analysis, and digital or simulation-based assessment formats.

title: Research Projects
text:
Completed Projects


Causal Evaluation — ITT & CACE Effects in Digital Interventions

Project: Secondary analysis of a Randomized Controlled Trial evaluating the digital empathy-training “Zirkus Empathico”.
Focus: Moving beyond the traditional Intention-to-Treat perspective to account for varied usage in home-based settings.

CACE Modeling: Estimating the Complier Average Causal Effect to isolate the effect of actual intervention dosage.
Robustness Checks: Handling missing data via Full Information Maximum Likelihood and conducting MNAR Tipping-Point Analyses to test the sensitivity of results against non-random attrition.

Key Insight: While ITT effects were delayed, CACE estimates revealed stronger potential effects for high-compliance users, while simultaneously highlighting the risks of causal interpretation when design assumptions are challenged.



Experimental Study — Epistemic Emotions & Information-Seeking

Project: An online experiment on the impact of linguistic framing in refutation texts.
Research Goal: Examining whether subtle emotional framing, especially confusion versus interest, influences how learners engage with scientific misinformation and exploratory click behavior.

Design: Between-subjects experiment with behavioral proxies for information-seeking.
Analysis: Regression models and robustness checks for emotion induction.
Reflection: A methodological critique of click-based behavioral measures and the limits of retrospective emotion reporting.




Bachelor’s Thesis — Mechanisms of Well-being

Title: Verwobene Wege zum Wohlbefinden: Achtsamkeit, Dezentrierung und emotionale Kompetenz.
Research Goal: Identifying the “how” and “why” behind the benefits of mindfulness by testing a mediation model.

Methods: Path analysis and mediation modeling using SPSS AMOS and PROCESS.
Insight: This project laid the foundation for my interest in protective factors, while highlighting the need for the advanced causal and longitudinal methods I now apply in my Master’s work.

title: Research Projects
text:
Technical & Open Science Commitment
All projects are conducted with a strong commitment to Open Science principles: * Reproducibility: Analysis scripts are written in R, utilizing Quarto for fully transparent and automated reporting. * Rigorous Modeling: Preference for latent variable models (SEM) to account for measurement error. * Causal Thinking: Explicit reflection on the assumptions (e.g., Exclusion Restriction in CACE) necessary for causal claims in educational research.

At the same time, I am interested in how socio-emotional and self-regulatory skills can be deliberately supported through educational interventions.
